schedule.name: NextEra Energy Resources 250
schedule.summary: Corey Heim won the Daytona opener after Christian Eckes — who had led 67 laps — was collected in a final-lap crash instigated by a lapped truck. Heim threaded through the carnage from fifth place to take the lead on the last corner of the last lap. One of the most chaotic Truck finishes in recent years.

driver_of_race.analysis: Won from fifth with one corner to go. Eckes had the dominant truck all race — Heim survived the carnage. That's Daytona.

story_cards.title: The Last Corner Win
story_cards.body: Corey Heim was running fifth with a quarter-mile left in the race. Christian Eckes led, looked untouchable, and was about to claim his first Daytona Trucks win. Then a lapped truck made contact at the trioval and triggered a chain-reaction crash that collected Eckes, the leader, and several trucks around him. Heim dodged the wreckage and crossed the line first by 0.028 seconds over Ben Rhodes. One of the most surreal Truck wins in years.

story_cards.title: Eckes Heartbreak
story_cards.body: Christian Eckes led 67 of 101 laps and was dominant from flag to the final quarter-mile. His ThorSport Ford had no equal in the draft. The lapped-truck contact wasn't his fault, but the Daytona superspeedway takes no prisoners. He left Daytona with zero points from the race result despite the most commanding performance of any driver all afternoon.

story_cards.title: GMS Racing's Depth
story_cards.body: Grant Enfinger and Rajah Caruth finished fourth and third respectively, giving GMS Racing a strong opening weekend result. Caruth — in just his second full Trucks season — showed maturity well beyond his years in navigating the final-lap chaos. GMS enters 2026 with genuine championship ambitions for the first time since 2021.

story_cards.title: 0.028 Seconds
story_cards.body: The margin of victory was the closest Trucks finish at Daytona since 2019. Ben Rhodes had the better run off Turn 4 and nearly got to Heim's bumper at the line — but 0.028 seconds is essentially one truck-length. The photo finish captured a moment that will define the 2026 Trucks season's opening chapter.

insights.headline: Eckes: The Daytona Tax
insights.body: Leading the most laps and crashing on the last corner is the most specifically Daytona outcome imaginable. Eckes' truck was genuinely the best machine on track. The superspeedway format is designed to punish dominance and reward survival. He now carries a 13-point deficit into Atlanta having showed the pace of a champion.

insights.headline: Heim Survives and Thrives
insights.body: Corey Heim's superspeedway IQ was evident in how he positioned himself for the final restart — far enough back to avoid the primary crash, fast enough to capitalise. His 2025 Trucks championship was built on this exact combination of patience and execution. He starts 2026 as he finished 2025: in Victory Lane.

insights.headline: Eckes Deficit Day One
insights.body: 13 points behind Heim after one race isn't a crisis — but it means Eckes needs to be perfect when Heim isn't. The ThorSport programme has the trucks to win multiple races; the question is whether Christian can avoid the one bad result that kills a championship campaign.

insights.headline: 0.028s — The Margin of Chaos
insights.body: The closest Trucks finish in six years came down to one corner, one lapped truck, and one chain reaction. That's the sport. That's Daytona. The 0.028-second margin means both Heim and Rhodes walk away feeling like they could have won or lost with one different decision. Neither is wrong.

schedule.name: NextBase 250
schedule.summary: Christian Eckes took full revenge for his Daytona heartbreak, leading 98 laps and winning Atlanta convincingly. A late caution that neutralised the field came at exactly the wrong time for Corey Heim, who had been conserving tyres for a final charge — Eckes held every restart and crossed the line 0.551 seconds clear.

story_cards.title: Eckes Redeemed
story_cards.body: Christian Eckes let Daytona go. Atlanta was his from the moment he took the lead on Lap 4 — he led 98 laps with an authority that matched his pace from the previous week. This time, there was no late-race chaos to rob him. He won by more than half a second in a race that was never particularly close once he found clean air.

insights.headline: The Revenge Win
insights.body: Leading 98 laps and winning at the second superspeedway in a row — after leading 67 laps and crashing at the first — is a statement. Eckes doesn't hide from the venues that hurt him. He comes back harder. That's a championship mentality.

schedule.name: Toyota Tundra 225
schedule.summary: Corey Heim converted pole into his second win of 2026 at COTA, beating a charging Ben Rhodes by 1.887 seconds. Christian Eckes finished fifth after a pit road speeding penalty dropped him from second place on Lap 38.

story_cards.title: Heim Takes Control
story_cards.body: Two wins, one second place — Corey Heim has the best start to a Trucks season since Kyle Busch dominated in 2010. His COTA performance was technically polished: clean lap times, smart tyre management, and no moments of risk. Eckes was on course for second before his penalty on Lap 38 dropped him to fifth and handed Heim a 13-point championship lead.

insights.headline: Heim in Command
insights.body: 13 points after three rounds. Heim leads Eckes (who should be ahead given his Atlanta win) by a margin that reflects the consistency gap between the two. Heim's four finishes of P1, P2, P1 versus Eckes' P21, P1, P5 tells the whole story. One bad result decides championships.
